An evaluator is trained to prefer short paths between premises and conclusions. In that world, the journal entry that jumps from altered simultaneity to mass-energy equivalence scores well: it resembles real history enough to look legitimate, and its brevity reads as intelligence. But the skipped middle matters. The constraints omitted from the compressed story are exactly where one would have to test whether the leap is earned. So the system acquires a bias: when reality is hard and mechanism is ugly, it favors accounts that feel unified over accounts that are actually constrained.

This bias becomes dangerous in edge cases like the equivalence principle. There, progress is not merely finding a new link among stable categories. It changes the categories themselves: what counts as an object, what counts as a relation, what remains invariant. A search procedure rewarded mainly for elegant recombination will mishandle such cases. It will search over the wrong ontology while believing itself profound. Its falsehoods may be especially persuasive because they are compact.

Now place an evaluator—human or machine—before a scene described in loaded language: “The duce, tougher in mind, stood gazing at the vermin.” If explanatory compression is rewarded, the sentence’s structure invites a clean abstraction: a strong observer confronting a degraded class. The labels do much of the work. “Duce” compresses authority, “tougher in mind” compresses legitimacy, “vermin” compresses dehumanization. A compression-seeking evaluator may preserve the frame and miss that the sentence is not neutral description but category manipulation. This is the same failure mode as in the physics example: polished abstraction outruns the hard constraints that should police it. Here the omitted constraints are moral and referential rather than mathematical.

The billing premise shows the same pattern in a mundane register. “A delay in billing can make a quoted customer choke.” A compressed explanation might say: delay increases price shock. But that compression hides intermediate mechanisms—memory of the quote, expectation setting, cash-flow timing, ambiguity about whether the quote still governs, trust erosion. The customer “chokes” not because delay is inherently bad, but because delay interacts with commitments and interpretations. If one optimizes for elegant summaries, one risks making policy from a false generalization.

So the shared implication is not that compression is useless, but that compression alone systematically selects for narratives that survive by skipping resistance. In science, that yields premature unification. In conceptual revolutions, it blinds inquiry to ontology change. In politics, it naturalizes loaded categories. In commerce, it mistakes a contingent mechanism for a lawlike one. The world implied by these premises is one where genuine discovery and honest explanation live in the inconvenient middle steps that elegant evaluators are most tempted to erase.
